interpretations.interpretation: Many would understand this as describing the suffering “servant” as a figure who truly bears the community’s sickness and pain—suggesting that the servant’s suffering corresponds to (and may bring relief from) the people’s afflictions.

Some could read it as a claim of mistaken popular judgment: the audience at the time had thought the sufferer was “struck by God” because of wrongdoing, but the text reframes this—saying the sufferer’s condition is not proof of divine punishment but of their role in bearing others’ burdens.

A hearer in the context of Israel’s history of covenant and purification might interpret this as an atonement-like message: the servant’s suffering is presented as the means by which God addresses the people’s sins and resulting suffering (a kind of substitutionary bearing of pain).

Others might understand “we…thought” and “he…carried” corporately: the suffering servant could be understood not as an individual Messiah but as Israel itself (or a representative righteous remnant), carrying the consequences that the nation has experienced.

Given the language of God’s involvement (“struck,” “God,” “answered,” “carrying pain”), some contemporary audiences could see this as a theologically loaded sign that God is acting through the servant’s suffering—moving from apparent rejection to vindication/relief.
